decisions.question: Event vocabulary, payload shape, and self-update treatment: what discrete events does the bus carry, what fields do they include, and does tsuku self-update use the same event family as tool updates?
decisions.notes: Drives every subscriber and every publisher. Includes the notices-subscriber reaction table (what file mutation each event causes). Trade-off space: minimal (Updated{tool,from,to,err}) vs expressive (Started/Succeeded/Failed/Rolled-Back/Removed/Cancelled with rich payloads). Self-update is structurally similar (tool ID 'tsuku') but lives outside state.json — needs an explicit answer.
decisions.chosen: Three events (Activated, InstallFailed, Removed) in new internal/installevents package; Source enum (SourceInstall, SourceManualUpdate, SourceAutoUpdate, SourceRollback, SourceSelf); Activate publishes only on state-change (rollback after failed install is a no-op state-wise, so it emits nothing); self-update reuses Activated with Tool="tsuku" and InstallFailed for new failure visibility; InboxReporter warnings (KindVersionFallback, KindShellInitChange) stay outside the bus.

decisions.question: Bus delivery semantics: synchronous (publisher blocks until all subscribers run) vs synchronous-with-recover (subscribers cannot panic the publisher) vs asynchronous (fire-and-forget on a goroutine).
decisions.notes: User constraint: notices remain best-effort, subscriber failure must not block install success. Includes sub-question of subscriber-ordering determinism and re-entrancy policy (subscriber publishing more events).
decisions.chosen: synchronous-with-recover; deterministic registration-order; queue-and-flush re-entrancy; errors swallowed-and-logged; named subscribers

decisions.question: Where does Publish() get called: a thin shim wrapping state.json mutations vs explicit Publish at each install/update/remove/rollback site vs defer-based instrumentation at function entry.
decisions.notes: Same outcome (event emitted) but vastly different maintainability and audit cost. State.json shim auto-covers all mutations but couples bus to storage layer; explicit calls are auditable but easy to forget; defer is clever but error-prone with early-return paths.
decisions.chosen: Explicit Publish at each lifecycle site (~6 functions: Manager.InstallWithOptions, Manager.Activate, Manager.RemoveVersion, Manager.RemoveAllVersions, auto-apply loop, Manager.Remove)
decisions.assumption_to_validate: Decision 1 chose lifecycle vocabulary (Installed/Activated/Removed/ApplyFailed), not state-mutation vocabulary. If state-mutation vocabulary won, option 1 becomes viable - re-evaluate.

decisions.question: Subscriber registration mechanism: single Register() call from cmd/tsuku/main.go (and equivalents like the auto-apply subprocess entrypoint) vs per-package init() functions that self-register vs explicit constructor-injected subscriber list.
decisions.notes: Trade-off: discoverability (one file vs scattered init) vs import cycles (does subscriber package need to import bus, or vice versa?). Affects how a reader answers 'what runs on an install-succeeded event?'
decisions.chosen: Single explicit wiring helper in cmd/tsuku (bus.New() + per-subscriber Subscribe(name, sub) calls in one file). Auto-apply subprocess is the same binary with a hidden subcommand, so single wiring point covers both entry points trivially.

cross_validation_notes: Decision 3 assumed vocabulary names (Installed/Activated/Removed/ApplyFailed); Decision 1 chose (Activated/InstallFailed/Removed). Different names, same publisher set. No restart.
Decision 1 introduced a Source enum that requires Manager.Install/Activate/Remove* signature changes (~10 call sites). Decision 3 didn't flag this. Documented in Decision Outcome.
Self-update path (updates.CheckAndApplySelf) is a 7th publisher in addition to the ~6 from Decision 3. Documented in Decision Outcome.
